The whole point of day 3 was geometric intuition: a 2×2 matrix bends, rotates, or stretches space. If you can't sketch what [[2,0],[0,1]] or [[0,-1],[1,0]] does to a unit square, the concept didn't land yet.

replies.feedback:
Good that you flagged the gap directly instead of bluffing. You are right to pause — matrices as transformations only makes sense once vectors and dot products feel concrete.

Let's back up. Before the next session, watch 3Blue1Brown "Essence of Linear Algebra" episodes 1 and 2 (about 20 minutes total). Then try this: write the vector [3, 2] as a combination of [1, 0] and [0, 1]. That single idea — vectors as instructions to scale basis arrows — is the foundation everything else sits on.

replies.reply:
Vector is basically the dynamic list or one dimensional array. 
Basically to get 3,2 vector I need to: 3*(1,0) =  3,0. And 2*(0,1) =0,2. And then I add up: 3,0 + 0,2 = 3,2 vector.
replies.feedback:
You correctly described how a vector can be built from scaled basis vectors — that is the linear combination idea, and it matters.

But the question was about eigenvectors, and you didn't touch them. An eigenvector of a matrix A is a vector whose direction doesn't change when A is applied — it only gets stretched or squished by some scalar (the eigenvalue). That is the whole point.

Try this: take the covariance matrix of a 2D dataset where points spread mostly along a diagonal. Ask yourself which direction, when you apply the covariance matrix to it, comes back pointing the same way. That direction is the first principal component. Now you can answer why PCA finds eigenvectors.
